event_blocks.summary: In a European city, likely Paris, American fast food chains like Five Guys, Popeyes, Krispy Kreme, McDonald's, and KFC are prominently featured, with a narrator observing their takeover of France. A man with an afro and sunglasses stands before a McDonald's, while French voices express appreciation for American fast food, citing a love for chicken from KFC. A woman eating at Five Guys describes fast food as a current trend that is addictive and brings happiness. Statistics reveal that 1,300 fast food stores opened in France between 2019 and 2023, generating €19 billion in revenue. Despite France's reputation for gastronomy, the French palate is reportedly changing, with France being McDonald's and Burger King's second-largest market outside the US. Xavier Expilly, President and Founder of EXPM, explains that France is an 'Eldorado' for these brands, offering significantly higher sales than other countries. The narrative concludes with a woman exiting a KFC with a branded bag and walking down a cobblestone street, passing a souvenir kiosk and a café.

McDonald’s restaurant.description: Multiple appearances, including one with yellow flowers above the entrance and another at dusk with festive lights and a 'Menu McFirst 5€' sign, noted by the narrator as part of France being McDonald's second largest market outside the US.

Man with afro and sunglasses.description: Stands in front of McDonald’s, wearing a brown jacket and carrying a backpack, with yellow flowers above the restaurant.

Krispy Kreme.description: Storefront with a circular logo and 'Original Glazed' text, featuring a clean, minimalist design, identified by the narrator as a 'recent arrival' to France.

KFC restaurant.description: Exterior with a red awning and Colonel Sanders logo, with a male voice expressing enthusiasm, saying 'KFC, of course, because I love chicken,' and later showing a woman carrying a KFC-branded paper bag.

Woman in pink coat.description: Walks out of KFC carrying a KFC paper bag, later seen walking down a cobblestone street.

Popeyes restaurant.description: Modern storefront with large glass windows and a yellow 'Popeyes' sign, advertising 'Famous Louisiana Chicken', identified by the narrator as a 'recent arrival' to France.

Gourmet dish.description: Elegantly plated with chicken, mashed potatoes, and garnishes, visually contrasting with the fast food imagery, as the narrator discusses the changing French palate.

Woman eating at Five Guys.description: Wearing a black hoodie with 'TRODHE' written on it, eating a meal with a Five Guys cup and fries, while a female voice with a French accent describes fast food as 'the hype,' 'the trend now,' 'addictive,' and bringing 'happiness to people,' likely reflecting her sentiment or that of a fellow patron.

Street scene with pedestrians.description: Cobblestone street with people walking, including a woman in a pink coat with a KFC bag, near a souvenir kiosk and café, forming a backdrop to the discussion of fast food's growth in France.

Five Guys restaurant.description: Exterior with red awnings and outdoor seating in a European city, later showing patrons eating inside, accompanied by upbeat, slightly jazzy background music.

Xavier Expilly.description: Appears on a laptop screen during an interview, identified as President and Founder of EXPM, wearing a light-colored shirt. He speaks with a French accent, initially remarking, 'We appreciate more American fast food than Americans themselves,' and later explaining that 'a lot of brands want to come in France because France is an Eldorado. You do two or three times sales more than the other country,' highlighting France's appeal to fast food brands.

raw_segments: The video opens with an exterior shot of a Five Guys restaurant in a European city, likely Paris, featuring red awnings and outdoor seating, accompanied by upbeat, slightly jazzy background music. A female narrator states, "American fast food is taking over France." The scene transitions to patrons eating inside Five Guys, then to modern storefronts of Popeyes and Krispy Kreme, which the narrator identifies as "recent arrivals." A young man with an afro and sunglasses stands before a McDonald’s adorned with yellow flowers, as a male voice with a French accent remarks, "We appreciate more American fast food than Americans themselves." This is followed by a KFC storefront, and another male voice with a French accent declares, "KFC, of course, because I love chicken." A woman eating at a Five Guys counter, wearing a black hoodie with 'TRODHE', is seen as a female voice with a French accent describes fast food as "the hype," "the trend now," and "addictive," bringing "happiness to people." The video cuts to a bustling street scene with fast food outlets, overlaid with text revealing that "1,300 fast food stores opened in France 2019-2023" and generated "€19b" in revenue, with the narrator confirming these figures. A gourmet dish is shown, contrasting with the fast food theme, as the narrator explains that "the French palate is changing" despite the country's reputation for gastronomy, adding that "France is McDonald's and Burger King's second largest market outside of the US." A McDonald’s is then shown at dusk with people entering and exiting, and a man eating a burger inside. A laptop screen displays an interview with Xavier Expilly, President and Founder of EXPM, who, with a French accent, explains that "a lot of brands want to come in France because France is an Eldorado. You do two or three times sales more than the other country." The segment concludes with a woman walking out of a KFC holding a branded bag, followed by a wide view of a cobblestone street where the woman in a pink coat, still carrying her KFC bag, walks past a souvenir kiosk and a café, as a final male voice with an American accent briefly mentions a "country that has a high pro..."

event_blocks.summary: Andrew Charles, Managing Director at TD Cowen, discussed the success of American brands and demographics in France, while a female narrator questioned how American fast-food companies appealed to the "culinary conscious, fine dining, Michelin star-loving French crowd." CNBC investigated in Paris, showing a Burger King restaurant on a bustling Parisian street scene during lunchtime in the Les Halles neighborhood, with a title card "Why the French love American fast food." A male interviewee, speaking French with an accent, expressed a preference for fries and nuggets, followed by visuals of a McDonald’s restaurant and a close-up of a McDonald’s burger, accompanied by a text overlay indicating a "61% increase in fast-food sales in France 2013-2023." A pie chart detailed American fast-food market shares in France for 2023, with McDonald’s leading at 34.5%, and the narrator highlighted McDonald's and Burger King's significant combined sales. A Quick restaurant was shown with a text overlay stating '47% fast-food sales in France from burgers in 2023,' emphasizing the role of burgers. Xavier Expilly, President and Founder of EXPM, asserted that "the burger has taken a growing share of the market over the last 30 years and has become an essential item on every restaurant menu," with visuals of people eating burgers and a vintage McDonald’s commercial. The narrator explained that American fast-food companies built brands behind iconic products like McDonald's Big Mac and Burger King's Whopper, as seen in a close-up of Burger King burgers. Joel Tissier, CEO of Domino’s Pizza France, noted that despite France being "the country of the Michelin restaurant," QSR fast food is "very trendy," and "we still understand that we love treating ourselves," illustrated by visuals of Domino’s pizza and people eating at KFC and McDonald’s. An animated bar chart showed "American fast-food companies in France by profit 2018," with McDonald’s, Burger King, KFC, Domino’s, and Subway outlets. The segment continued with an animated infographic for "American fast-food companies in France by profit 2023," showing increased profits and store openings, with Charles explaining the success of "affordable indulgence." Expilly emphasized France's "huge potential" for American brands, while visuals shifted to a Popeyes restaurant and traditional French bistros, contrasting with the "55% of France’s restaurants are traditional full-service." The narrator praised France's culinary leadership, mentioning Michelin stars and Cordon Bleu, before showing a KFC restaurant and a man eating inside, noting France had 157,000 eateries in 2022. The narrative then shifted to a nighttime exterior of a Popeyes, with Charles discussing QSR concepts focusing on modernized images and higher quality, illustrated by interiors of Five Guys, and visuals of agricultural machinery and a poultry farm, highlighting French ingredient sourcing for products like McDonald's fries and Popeyes' chicken sandwiches. Expilly stressed the need for emotion and connection, and strong partnerships with farmers, followed by KFC and McDonald’s exteriors and a close-up of McDonald’s fries, concluding with a vintage McDonald’s commercial and the narrator stating QSRs adapted to EU food standards. The segment then showed a modern McDonald’s, a close-up of card payment, and gourmet pizzas, with the narrator explaining menu adaptations to cater to French palates, such as Domino’s 'raclette' campaign, leading consumers to consider chains "as much French as American." A male speaker echoed this sentiment, stating brands like McDonald's and KFC feel "part of us now," with visuals of queues outside McDonald’s and a Krispy Kreme storefront. A historical note marked "50 years ago: In 1972, the first McDonald’s in France opened in Créteil," over a billboard, with Charles confirming McDonald's "first quick-service concept to go to France and found tremendous success there." The narrative then focused on Burger King's re-entry into France in 2012, its second-highest number of QSR franchises, and a bar chart showing its 502 locations, alongside entry years for Pizza Hut, KFC, Subway, and Domino’s, and the recent openings of Popeyes and Krispy Kreme, with a woman expressing enthusiasm for "new brands in France." Visuals included a McDonald’s self-service kiosk, Expilly discussing digitization, and the flourishing of the fast-food industry through "takeaway, drive-thru, pick and collect, delivery," supported by images of delivery cyclists and a McDonald’s drive-thru. The segment concluded with a close-up of a hand passing a bag through a car window, Domino’s Pizza exterior with delivery scooters, and Tissier explaining that "less than 10% of our orders" are at the counter, with "60% are online." The narrator highlighted that "One in five meals in France is eaten outside the home," with predictions of an increase, followed by visuals of a Domino’s counter, a woman eating inside, and Charles discussing "strong unit economics" due to a "young population, high immigration population," and "high GDP per capita country," adding that "America's pop culture influence is another powerful tool." A close-up of a 5 Euro note and receipt appeared, followed by a street scene and a woman in a gray coat discussing how young people grew up watching American series and movies, eating fast food, and were "pretty excited" when fast food came to France. The narrator noted "Bigger portions and modest presentations appeal to even the most delicate palate," and that "High inflation and shortened lunch breaks have contributed to a need for convenience," as visuals showed a bustling Burger King interior with customers, including two women eating burgers and fries, with Tissier describing American food as "tasty, big."

McDonald’s restaurant.description: Self-service kiosk with a touchscreen displaying French text 'TOUCHEZ POUR COMMANDER' (Touch to order), and later a drive-thru sign reading 'COMMANDEZ ICI' (Order Here) with cars lined up. Xavier Expilly, speaking with a French accent, directly references 'takeaway' and 'drive-thru' as key aspects of modern QSR operations, aligning with these visuals.

Andrew Charles.description: Managing Director at TD Cowen, wearing a dark suit, white shirt, and headset, speaking in a professional indoor setting with white wainscoting and windows, with an American accent, discussing a "young population, high immigration population," and a "high GDP per capita country that leads to strong unit economics."

Text overlay: '47% fast-food sales in Fr.description: Yellow box with white text, overlaid on a shot of the Quick restaurant exterior, introduced as a statistic by a female narrator.

KFC restaurant.description: Exterior with black and red signage, located on a Parisian street corner with a 'Boulevard Sébastopol' street sign visible, with people walking past and a bicycle parked nearby.

Burger King restaurant.description: Exterior with red awning and 'Burger King' sign, and interior with customers entering and eating, including a woman with curly hair and a green shirt, and another with a pink scarf, seated at a table with a red tray, fries, and orange juice.

Man in green jacket and glasses.description: Standing in front of McDonald’s, wearing a green jacket, glasses, and carrying a backpack, holding a McDonald’s bag and looking upward.

Quick restaurant.description: Exterior with a red sign displaying a white 'Q' and burger logo, located on a Parisian street corner with a 'Rue de la Reine' street sign visible, shown as the narrator states that 47% of fast-food sales in France come from burgers.

Street scene with pedestrians.description: Cobblestone street in Paris with people walking, including a woman in a gray coat and scarf speaking in front of Burger King, and others walking past a bar with a French menu on the glass.

Domino’s Pizza.description: Exterior with 'A emporter' and 'En livraison' signs, blue delivery scooters parked outside, and interior with black-tiled wall featuring the Domino’s logo.

Man in dark coat eating at KFC.description: Seated inside a KFC restaurant, wearing a dark coat, eating a meal with a drink in front of him.

Close-up of two burgers.description: Two large burgers with sesame seed buns, layered with patties, cheese, and lettuce, placed on a white surface with Burger King packaging visible, mentioned by a male speaker with a French accent as 'the Whopper' for Burger King.

Woman exiting McDonald’s.description: Holding a McDonald’s drink cup, wearing a beige coat, walking out of the restaurant with a man beside her, shown as Joel Tissier discusses the appeal of fast food.

Hand placing pizza box.description: A hand placing a Domino’s pizza box on a table, with a blue and white checkered tablecloth in the background.

Vintage television.description: Old-style CRT television displaying a grainy McDonald’s commercial with a man smiling and holding a bag.

Xavier Expilly.description: Appears on a laptop screen during an interview, identified as President and Founder of EXPM, wearing a light-colored shirt. He speaks with a French accent, discussing how 'takeaway, drive-thru, pick and collect, delivery' are now possible for QSRs, calling it a 'perfect match for today needs with the hybridization of concepts, accelerating.'

Joel Tissier.description: CEO of Domino’s Pizza France, wearing a dark suit and white shirt, speaking indoors with a window behind him, with a French accent, discussing how only "less than 10% of our orders" are at the counter, with "60% are online," and later describing American food as "tasty, big."

Woman with curly hair and glasses.description: Eating a burger, wearing a dark coat and glasses, with curly hair, seated at a table, seen as a female narrator states, 'But it's not only burgers.'

American fast-food companies in France b.description: Animated bar chart showing McDonald’s (1,464 outlets), Burger King (281), KFC (240), Domino’s (387), and Subway (450), with colorful building icons and a blue background, introduced by a female narrator discussing top-performing companies.

Animated bar chart with colorful building icons representing McDonald’s, Burger King, KFC, Domino’s, and Subway, showing outlet numbers for 2023, with a blue background and source cited as Euromonitor International, visually complementing the female narrator's statistics on store openings and profit increases.

Popeyes restaurant.description: Modern storefront with large glass windows and a yellow 'Popeyes' sign, advertising 'Famous Louisiana Chicken', with people entering and exiting, including a man in a white beanie and scarf holding a Popeyes bag, and its chicken sandwiches are mentioned as using locally sourced ingredients.

Laptop setup.description: A black laptop on a wooden desk, displaying Xavier Expilly on screen, with a white CNBC-branded mug beside it and a green plant in the background, serving as the platform for Xavier Expilly's interview.

Potato harvester.description: A red and yellow machine pulling a trailer, loading freshly harvested potatoes onto a conveyor belt, with a blue tractor attached, harvesting potatoes that are used for McDonald's French fries.

Poultry farm.description: Large indoor facility with thousands of chicks under automated feeders, with red and blue feeding systems suspended from the ceiling, raising chicken used in Popeyes' sandwiches.

Combine harvester.description: Aerial view of a green combine harvester moving through a golden wheat field, kicking up dust as it cuts and collects the crop.

Five Guys restaurant.description: Interior with red and white checkered walls, employees in red shirts behind the counter, and customers ordering milkshakes.

TikTok video montage.description: Split-screen clips showing Domino’s Raclette pizza being prepared and eaten, with user handles like @beyaz_korpe and @deborah_maaaz, and French text overlays such as 'La *raclette*' and 'Ca sent TROP bon!'. These visuals directly support Joel Tissier's discussion of the successful Raclette campaign.

Historical billboard.description: Roadside billboard with McDonald’s logo and the text '50 years ago: In 1972, the first McDonald’s in France opened in Créteil,' attributed to Le Parisien, August 8, 2022. This visual provides context for McDonald's long-standing presence in France, which is further discussed by the narrator and Andrew Charles.

Krispy Kreme.description: Storefront with a circular logo and a woman in a yellow coat eating a red-frosted donut inside. The female narrator mentions Krispy Kreme opened its first French store in February 2024, and a female voice with a French accent expresses that 'it's a new brand in France and it's very, very fantastic for the French people.'

Los Angeles Times article.description: Torn-paper reveal showing a headline 'Burger King tries again in France, land of gastronomy' with an image of a Whopper sandwich. A female narrator provides context, explaining Burger King's re-entry into France after a 15-year hiatus.

Woman in yellow coat.description: Eating a red-frosted donut inside a Krispy Kreme, wearing a yellow coat and a gold necklace, with a purple circular light in the background.

Drive-thru sign.description: Black vertical sign with white text 'COMMANDEZ ICI' (Order Here) and an arrow pointing right, with cars waiting in line. This visual is directly referenced by Xavier Expilly's audio mention of 'drive-thru' as a modern QSR service.

Delivery cyclists.description: Wearing bright green jackets and black beanies, with teal thermal delivery bags mounted on their bicycles. Their presence is contextualized by Xavier Expilly's audio mention of 'delivery' as a key service.

Bar chart: American fast-food restaurant.description: Ranked list showing McDonald’s (1,547), Burger King (502), Domino’s Pizza (479), Subway (378), and KFC (335), with Burger King highlighted in yellow. A female narrator confirms Burger King 'boasts the second highest number of American QSR franchises in the country.'

Animated map of France.description: Blue silhouette of France with logos of American fast-food chains (Pizza Hut, KFC, Subway, Domino’s) appearing with their entry years. A female narrator explicitly names these chains and their entry years (Pizza Hut 1987, KFC 1991, Subway 2001, Domino's 2006), confirming the visual information.

Woman with pink scarf.description: Seated at a table inside Burger King, eating a burger, wearing a pink scarf and a dark jacket, next to the woman with curly hair.

Woman in gray coat and scarf.description: Standing in front of Burger King, speaking and gesturing, wearing a gray coat and a patterned scarf, with the red Burger King sign visible behind her, speaking with a French accent about young people growing up watching American series and movies, eating fast food, and being "pretty excited" when fast food came to France.

Woman with braided hair and glasses.description: Eating at a table inside Domino’s, using a fork, wearing a patterned sweater, with a red tray and glass of water in front of her.

Woman with curly hair and green shirt.description: Seated at a table inside Burger King, eating a burger and fries, wearing a green shirt and a white jacket, with a glass of orange juice in front of her.

raw_segments: The segment opens with Andrew Charles, Managing Director at TD Cowen, speaking in a professional indoor setting, wearing a headset and suit, discussing the success of American brands and demographics in France. A female narrator then questions how American fast-food companies won over the "culinary conscious, fine dining, Michelin star-loving French crowd," stating that CNBC visited Paris to investigate. Upbeat, light background music with a French feel begins, accompanying a transition to a bustling Parisian street scene. We see a Burger King with a red awning and outdoor seating, where pedestrians, including a person in a yellow jacket, walk by. The camera then captures a busy cobblestone street lined with shops and cafes, including a Heineken-branded café, as people walk in both directions, with ambient street sounds audible. The narrator identifies this as lunchtime in the Les Halles neighborhood. A title card, 'Why the French love American fast food,' appears, followed by a scenic view of the Seine River with runners and historic buildings. Over this, a male interviewee, speaking French with an accent, explains his preference for fries and nuggets. The video then shows a McDonald’s restaurant with yellow flowers above the entrance, where a man in a green jacket and glasses stands holding a McDonald’s bag. A close-up of a McDonald’s burger appears with a text overlay: '61% increase in fast-food sales in France 2013-2023,' as the narrator confirms this significant rise. A pie chart follows, detailing American fast-food market shares in France for 2023, with McDonald’s leading at 34.5%, Burger King at 9.1%, KFC at 4.1%, Domino’s Pizza at 1.9%, Subway at 1.2%, and 'Others' at 49.1%, while the narrator highlights McDonald's and Burger King as major players with combined sales of 8.3 billion euros. The scene shifts to a Quick restaurant with a red sign and burger logo, accompanied by a text overlay stating '47% fast-food sales in France from burgers in 2023,' with the narrator emphasizing the big role burgers play. The overall atmosphere transitions from professional interview to vibrant urban life, using visual and statistical evidence to underscore the popularity of American fast food in France, all set to the continuous background music.

The segment opens on a bustling cobblestone street in Paris, featuring a Quick restaurant, with a yellow text overlay stating '47% fast-food sales in France from burgers in 2023', as a female narrator explains that burgers accounted for about half of the country's sales that year. The scene transitions to a laptop screen where Xavier Expilly, President and Founder of EXPM, speaking with a French accent, asserts that "the burger has taken a growing share of the market over the last 30 years and has become an essential item on every restaurant menu." This is followed by a close-up of people eating burgers, including a woman with curly hair and glasses taking a bite, as the narrator adds, "But it's not only burgers." A vintage television then displays a grainy McDonald’s commercial, while the narrator explains that "American fast-food companies, helped by clever marketing, built their brands behind iconic products." The view cuts to a KFC storefront on a Parisian street, with a woman inside eating, as the male speaker with a French accent continues, "People in France want to discover other tastes and when you explain that with a good story with an iconic burger, you succeed," before listing iconic items like "Popeyes is a chicken sandwich. For McDonald's it's a Big Mac. For Burger King it's a Whopper." A close-up of two large burgers with sesame seed buns, clearly from Burger King packaging, accompanies this commentary. Joel Tissier, CEO of Domino’s Pizza France, then appears speaking indoors, explaining in clear English that despite France being "the country of the Michelin restaurant" with "gastronomy, very high standards," QSR fast food is "very trendy," showing that "even though we appreciate food, we still understand that we love treating ourselves." This commentary accompanies a hand placing a Domino’s pizza box on a table, a close-up of a Domino’s pizza being sliced, a man eating inside a KFC, and a woman exiting a McDonald’s holding a drink. The segment concludes with an animated bar chart titled 'American fast-food companies in France by profit 2018', displaying outlet numbers for McDonald’s, Burger King, KFC, Domino’s, and Subway, as the female narrator begins to discuss "the country's top performing..." before the audio cuts off.

The segment opens with an animated infographic titled 'American fast-food companies in France by profit 2023,' displaying colorful bar charts for McDonald’s, Burger King, KFC, Domino’s, and Subway outlets, while a female narrator explains that these fast-food chains (excluding Subway) opened nearly 500 stores across France, with total profits increasing by about 10%. Andrew Charles, Managing Director at TD Cowen, is then seen speaking in a professional indoor setting, detailing how the fast-casual concept has "heated up" in France, with American concepts successfully offering "affordable indulgence." While Charles remains on screen, a male voice with a noticeable French accent is heard, emphasizing France's "huge potential" for both pizza and American brands due to customer appetite. Upbeat acoustic guitar music begins as the video transitions to a modern Popeyes restaurant, showing people entering and exiting, including a man holding a Popeyes bag. The scene then shifts to a Parisian street with traditional French bistros like 'Le Grizzi' and 'Le Parvis,' featuring outdoor seating and red awnings, with a text overlay stating '55% of France’s restaurants are traditional full-service.' The female narrator continues, praising France as a global leader in culinary excellence, boasting more Michelin-starred restaurants and esteemed institutions like the Cordon Bleu. The scene then shows a KFC restaurant on a Parisian street corner with black and red signage and a 'Boulevard Sébastopol' street sign visible, concluding with a man in a dark coat eating inside a KFC, as the narrator adds that in 2022, France had 157,000 eateries, with over half being traditional full-service restaurants, attributing this to the country's culinary legacy, all accompanied by the continuous upbeat acoustic guitar music.

The minute opens with a nighttime exterior of a Popeyes restaurant, its vibrant red neon sign and fried chicken graphic reflected in large glass windows, as a man eats inside and a bicycle is parked outside. A female narrator explains how American quick-service restaurants (QSRs) took a specialized approach when entering the French market. The scene shifts to a bustling daytime outdoor café in a European city, then to Andrew Charles, Managing Director at TD Cowen, in a dark suit and headset, speaking in a professional indoor setting. He discusses the cliché of France being a fancier place to eat, noting how QSR concepts focused on modernized images, higher quality, higher price points, and more innovative offerings. The interior of a Five Guys restaurant with red and white checkered walls and employees serving milkshakes is shown, followed by an aerial view of a combine harvester in a golden wheat field and a close-up of a potato harvester loading potatoes onto a truck. An indoor poultry farm with thousands of chicks under automated feeders appears. The narrator highlights that this includes sourcing ingredients from French farmers, like the potatoes used for McDonald's French fries and the chicken in Popeyes' sandwiches. Xavier Expilly, President and Founder of EXPM, is seen on a laptop screen with a CNBC mug, speaking about how French people "don't get fed, we eat" and treat food with emotion, emphasizing the need for American brands to bring emotion and connection. He further stresses the importance of building strong partnerships with farmers for QSR American brands to succeed. The video then shows a KFC restaurant exterior in Paris with its Colonel Sanders sign, a modern McDonald’s building, and a close-up of McDonald’s fries. The segment concludes with a vintage CRT television displaying a grainy, flickering McDonald’s commercial, as the narrator states that American QSRs have adapted to European Union food standards, resulting in successful products.

The segment opens with an exterior shot of a modern McDonald’s restaurant in France, showing customers dining through large glass windows, followed by a close-up of a card payment for a McDonald's meal. The scene then displays four gourmet-style square pizzas, suggesting menu adaptations as a female narrator explains how brands cater to French palates by altering menu items or creating new products. Joel Tissier, CEO of Domino’s Pizza France, is then seen in an office setting, speaking with a French accent about the importance of being 'French friendly' and highlighting their 'raclette' campaign as their most successful. This is visually reinforced by a promotional image of Domino’s Raclette pizza and a split-screen TikTok montage showcasing its preparation and consumption with French text overlays like 'Authentique RACLETTE' and 'Ca sent TROP bon!'. The narrator notes these adaptations have paid off, with consumers now considering chains as much French as American. A male speaker with an accent, amidst background street noise, echoes this sentiment, stating that American brands like McDonald's and KFC feel like 'part of us now' and are 'everywhere.' The video shows people queuing outside a Parisian McDonald’s and a couple walking past a Krispy Kreme storefront. A historical note appears, marking '50 years ago: In 1972, the first McDonald’s in France opened in Créteil,' over a roadside billboard. The narrator then explains McDonald's has the strongest foothold due to its first-mover advantage, as Andrew Charles, Managing Director at TD Cowen, clearly states that McDonald's was the 'first quick-service concept to go to France and found tremendous success there,' while speaking in a professional setting.

The segment opens with an animated blue Burger King restaurant, transitioning to a torn-paper effect revealing a Los Angeles Times article about Burger King's expansion into France. A female narrator explains that while many have tried to replicate success, Burger King re-entered France after a 15-year hiatus in 2012, now boasting the second-highest number of American QSR franchises, a fact reinforced by a bar chart titled 'American fast-food restaurants in France' highlighting Burger King's 502 locations. The scene shifts to a blue animated map of France, where the narrator details the entry years of other chains: Pizza Hut in 1987, KFC in 1991, Subway in 2001, and Domino’s in 2006, noting their rapid expansion in the past decade. The narrator then mentions Popeyes and Krispy Kreme opening their first French stores in 2023 and 2024, as the video shows a real-world Krispy Kreme storefront and a woman in a yellow coat eating a red-frosted donut inside. A female voice with a French accent expresses enthusiasm, stating these are 'new brands in France' and 'very, very fantastic for the French people.' The video then shows a McDonald’s self-service kiosk with a hand interacting with the touchscreen displaying 'TOUCHEZ POUR COMMANDER,' followed by Xavier Expilly, President and Founder of EXPM, speaking on a laptop screen. The narrator explains that shifts in French society like digitization have allowed the fast-food industry to flourish. Expilly, speaking with a French accent, elaborates on these changes, mentioning 'takeaway, drive-thru, pick and collect, delivery,' and how 'all the QSR restaurant can do that,' calling it a 'perfect match for today needs with the hybridization of concepts, accelerating.' This aligns with visuals of delivery cyclists with teal thermal bags and a McDonald’s drive-thru sign reading 'COMMANDEZ ICI' with cars lined up.

The video opens with a close-up of a hand passing a white plastic bag through a car window, suggesting a drive-thru transaction, with a blurred car and taillight in the foreground. The scene transitions to the exterior of a Domino’s Pizza restaurant in France, where customers are entering and exiting, and blue delivery scooters are parked outside; the signage includes 'A emporter' (To go) and 'En livraison' (Delivery). Inside, Joel Tissier, CEO of Domino’s Pizza France, is interviewed, wearing a dark suit and white shirt, seated in a professional setting with a window behind him, explaining in a French accent that only "less than 10% of our orders" are at the counter, with "60% are online." A female narrator then highlights that "One in five meals in France is eaten outside the home," with experts predicting an increase. The camera then shows a customer placing a pizza box on a counter inside the Domino’s, with the brand’s logo on a black-tiled wall, followed by a woman with braided hair and glasses eating at a table inside the restaurant. Andrew Charles, Managing Director at TD Cowen, is then seen speaking in a professional indoor setting, wearing a dark suit, white shirt, and headset, discussing in an American accent a "young population, high immigration population," and a "high GDP per capita country that leads to strong unit economics." The narrator adds that "America's pop culture influence is another powerful tool." A close-up follows of a 5 Euro note and a receipt on a wooden table, with a glass of water beside it. The video then cuts to a street scene in Paris with cobblestone pavement, where pedestrians walk past a bar with a French menu. The camera then focuses on a woman in a gray coat and patterned scarf standing in front of a Burger King restaurant, speaking and gesturing with a French accent about how young people grew up watching American series and movies, eating fast food, and were "pretty excited" when fast food came to France. The narrator then notes that "Bigger portions and modest presentations appeal to even the most delicate palate," and that "High inflation and shortened lunch breaks have contributed to a need for convenience." The scene transitions to the bustling Burger King interior, where customers are seen entering, and two women are seated at a table, one with curly hair and a green shirt eating a burger and fries, while the other, wearing a pink scarf, eats a burger. Joel Tissier's voice returns, describing American food as "tasty, big."

event_blocks.summary: A man with an afro and sunglasses, wearing a white puffer jacket, green t-shirt, and beaded necklace, speaks to the camera outside a KFC restaurant. The scene shifts to a McDonald's at dusk, where a man walks by as a voice explains that lunchtime delays encourage out-of-home consumption. Simultaneously, a laptop displays Xavier Expilly, President and Founder of EXPM, during a CNBC-interview, discussing how American QSR brands use digital tools to improve consumer quality of life, with a CNBC mug visible. Inside a McDonald's, customers use self-service kiosks labeled 'TOUCHEZ POUR COMMANDER'. Outside a Subway, a man in a leather jacket gestures while speaking, as a voice expresses a preference for fast food during working hours due to budget constraints for high-quality French food on weekends. The video then shows a French café with outdoor seating, followed by a Burger King storefront with upbeat music. A McDonald's sign indicates closure until further notice on March 16, 2020. The final scenes reveal a McDonald's storefront marked with red handprints and protest signs reading 'URGENCE PALESTINE BOYCOTT ISRAEL' and 'BOYCOTT CETTE ENSEIGNE', accompanied by a CNBC overlay stating 'McDonald’s and Starbucks blame the Israel-Hamas war for slower sales — and the recovery might take a while'. The segment concludes with a close-up of the defaced McDonald's sign and a female voice explaining the restaurant industry's volatility and susceptibility to global changes, noting that McDonald's and Starbucks executives cited boycotts related to the war in Gaza for Q4 2023 sales slumps.

McDonald’s restaurant.description: Self-service kiosk with a touchscreen displaying French text 'TOUCHEZ POUR COMMANDER' (Touch to order), and later a drive-thru sign reading 'COMMANDEZ ICI' (Order Here) with cars lined up. Also shown as a storefront at dusk, a location with a 'THE GOVERNMENT REQUEST TO CLOSE UNTIL FURTHER NOTICE' sign dated March 16, 2020, and later defaced with red handprints and protest signs, while a female voice discusses boycotts affecting its sales.

Man with afro and sunglasses.description: A young man with an afro, wearing a white puffer jacket over a green t-shirt and a beaded necklace, seated at a table in front of a KFC restaurant, speaking directly to the camera with a non-native English accent, saying 'so that you will be...'. (Note: Sunglasses are not visible in this segment).

Subway restaurant.description: Exterior with green awning and yellow 'SUBWAY' sign, with a man in a leather jacket and cap speaking in front of it, while a male voice (not the man's own) discusses preferring fast food during working hours.

Protest signs on McDonald’s.description: Red and white signs with text 'URGENCE PALESTINE BOYCOTT ISRAEL' and 'BOYCOTT CETTE ENSEIGNE', affixed to a McDonald’s storefront, some with red paint resembling blood, appearing as a female voice discusses boycotts related to the war in Gaza.

KFC restaurant.description: Exterior with black and red signage, located on a Parisian street corner with a 'Boulevard Sébastopol' street sign visible, with people walking past and a bicycle parked nearby, serving as the backdrop for the young man speaking.

Burger King restaurant.description: Exterior with red awning and 'Burger King' sign, and interior with customers entering and eating, including a woman with curly hair and a green shirt, and another with a pink scarf, seated at a table with a red tray, fries, and orange juice, appearing during a segment with upbeat background music.

Laptop setup.description: A black laptop on a wooden desk, displaying Xavier Expilly on screen, with a white CNBC-branded mug beside it and a green plant in the background, serving as the visual context for a male voice discussing digital tools.

CNBC news headline overlay.description: White text on a dark background reading 'McDonald’s and Starbucks blame the Israel-Hamas war for slower sales — and the recovery might take a while', attributed to Amelia Lucas, February 5, 2024, appearing while a female voice discusses the restaurant industry's volatility and boycotts.

Defaced McDonald’s sign.description: The McDonald’s logo with red handprints and paint splatters, appearing as if bloodied, with a darkened storefront in the background, shown as a female voice discusses the impact of boycotts on sales.

Xavier Expilly.description: Appears on a laptop screen during an interview, identified as President and Founder of EXPM, wearing a light-colored shirt, while a male voice (not his own) discusses digital tools to improve consumer quality of life.

raw_segments: The video opens with a young man with an afro, wearing a white puffer jacket over a green t-shirt and a beaded necklace, seated at a table in front of a KFC restaurant, speaking directly to the camera with a non-native English accent, saying "so that you will be...". The scene quickly shifts to a McDonald’s storefront at dusk, where a man in a black jacket and cap walks past, while a male voice with a non-native English accent discusses how "lunchtime today is Arwin 42 minutes. This prevents people from going home and therefore encourage out of home consumption." This voice continues as a laptop screen displays Xavier Expilly, President and Founder of EXPM, during a video interview, with a CNBC-branded mug beside the laptop, explaining how "The QSR American brands respond perfectly to this problem with the development of digital tools to improve consumer's quality of life." The camera then moves inside a McDonald’s, showing customers using self-service kiosks with French text 'TOUCHEZ POUR COMMANDER' (Touch to order). Outside, a man in a leather jacket and cap stands in front of a Subway restaurant, gesturing as he speaks, while a new male voice with a non-native English accent states, "I really like good food. But you need a budget for that. So when I want to have really good French food, I I take my time, it's uh usually on weekend, during my working time. I prefer to go to to fast food." The video transitions to a cozy French café with outdoor seating, then to a Burger King storefront with its iconic logo, accompanied by upbeat, light background music. A McDonald’s location is then shown with a sign stating 'THE GOVERNMENT REQUEST TO CLOSE UNTIL FURTHER NOTICE' dated March 16, 2020. The final scenes depict a McDonald’s storefront covered in red-painted handprints and protest signs reading 'URGENCE PALESTINE BOYCOTT ISRAEL' and 'BOYCOTT CETTE ENSEIGNE', with a CNBC news headline overlay stating 'McDonald’s and Starbucks blame the Israel-Hamas war for slower sales — and the recovery might take a while'. The segment ends with a close-up of the McDonald’s sign defaced with red paint resembling blood. Throughout these later visuals, the upbeat background music continues as a female voice with a clear, professional tone explains, "But the restaurant industry is volatile and highly susceptible to domestic and global changes. In 2024, McDonald's and Starbucks executives said that boycotts related to the war in Gaza caused Q4 2023 sales to slump in countries with..."

event_blocks.summary: In Paris, protests against fast-food chains like McDonald's, marked by signs such as 'BOYCOTT ISRAEL' and 'URGENCE PALESTINE', and a Change.org petition against a Burger King, highlight concerns over environmental practices and the fast-food industry's expansion, coinciding with new French laws targeting single-use packaging and waste. Despite these challenges, the French fast-food market, projected to reach 21.5 billion euros by 2028, shows potential for growth, with chains like Chipotle having only six locations and Taco Bell not yet entering the market. Andrew Charles, Managing Director at TD Cowen, notes a near-term risk of macro-economic factors impacting restaurant traffic, while Joel Tissier, CEO of Domino’s Pizza France, discusses competing with larger chains and making their product broadly available with close to 500 stores. Industry experts observe a trend towards chicken-focused menus for higher margins and a younger demographic, with Steak 'n Shake boasting 37 locations and Carl’s Jr. and Wendy’s planning further expansion. McDonald's, which blamed weaker performance on a "pricing backlash," faces competition from self-service kiosks and the digital age, while Subway has closed over 100 French storefronts since 2015. The "French palate seems ready for it," as KFC meals are enjoyed, with customers noting a "special touch" that differentiates the taste.

McDonald’s restaurant.description: Exterior with graffiti and protest signs, including 'BOYCOTT ISRAEL', and a graphic overlay stating '7% France’s share of McDonald’s total revenue'; interior with self-service kiosks displaying French text 'TOUCHEZ POUR COMMANDER' and 'VOS BURGERS PRÉFÉRÉS, ÉGALEMENT DISPONIBLES AVEC DU BACON'. Joel Tissier refers to it as a competitor 'seven and twelve times bigger' than Domino's.

Domino’s Pizza.description: Exterior with its red and blue logo, followed by an employee placing a 'MEDIUM' pizza box on a counter, while a male voice with a French accent discusses making their product 'broadly available,' having 'close to 500 stores,' and mentioning 'the pizza distributor'.

Subway restaurant.description: Exterior with green and yellow 'SUBWAY' sign, with people entering and exiting, seen as a male voice with a French accent comments on 'stringflation' and a narrator states Subway closed over 100 French storefronts since 2015.

Protest signs on McDonald’s.description: Red and white signs with text 'BOYCOTT ISRAEL' and 'BOYCOTT CETTE ENSEIGNE' affixed to a McDonald’s storefront, some with red paint resembling blood, seen as a narrator discusses France's contribution to the fast-food chain's revenue and McDonald's blaming weaker performance on 'pricing backlash'.

Andrew Charles.description: Managing Director at TD Cowen, wearing a dark suit, white shirt, and headset, seated in a well-lit room, speaking to the camera about the industry's 'focus on chicken' for 'higher margin' and a 'younger consumer focused' strategy.

Joel Tissier.description: CEO of Domino’s Pizza France, wearing a dark suit and white shirt, speaking indoors with a window behind him, with a clear, professional male voice discussing Domino's market position and strategy.

Woman in green headscarf and mask.description: Wearing a green and white patterned headscarf and a green face mask, standing in a protest crowd with a Palestinian flag visible, seen as a narrator discusses France's 'high Muslim population'.

Protest sign against McDonald’s.description: White banner with a red 'X' over the McDonald’s logo and French text 'Non au fast food géant dans la rue Montorgueil!', hanging from a green metal archway, reflecting public sentiment against fast food expansion.

Woman in gray coat and scarf.description: Standing in front of Burger King, speaking and gesturing, wearing a gray coat and a patterned scarf, with the red Burger King sign visible behind her.

Burger King restaurant.description: Exterior with red awning and 'Burger King' sign, and interior with customers entering and eating, including a woman with curly hair and a green shirt, and another with a pink scarf, seated at a table with a red tray, fries, and orange juice. It is mentioned by Joel Tissier as a competitor and by a female narrator as the subject of protests in Paris over 'outdated environmental practices'.

Woman with curly hair and green shirt.description: Seated at a table inside KFC, eating a burger and fries, wearing a green shirt under a white jacket, with a glass of orange juice in front of her.

Woman with pink scarf.description: Seated beside the woman with curly hair, eating a burger, wearing a dark jacket and a pink scarf, with a glass of orange juice.

The Guardian article headline.description: Text overlay reading 'Ban on single-use restaurant tableware hailed as fast-food ‘revolution’ in France', attributed to Angelique Chrisafis, December 28, 2022, highlighting new regulations.

Change.org petition.description: Online petition titled 'Un Burger King place Martin Nadaud, Paris 20' with 24,932 signatures, started by Collectif Place Martin Nadaud, displayed on a screen with a photo of the location, quoted by a female narrator as protesting 'outdated environmental practices'.

Woman with braided hair and glasses.description: Eating at a table inside Domino’s, using a fork, wearing a patterned sweater, with a red tray and glass of water in front of her.

Woman eating at Five Guys.description: Seated at a table, holding a Five Guys drink cup with a straw, wearing a gray hoodie, with a red and white checkered interior visible in the background.

Five Guys restaurant.description: Interior with red and white checkered walls, employees in red shirts behind the counter, and customers ordering milkshakes, also showing a woman eating at a table, holding a Five Guys drink cup.

Woman with curly hair and glasses.description: Eating a burger at a Burger King outdoor table, wearing a dark coat and glasses, with curly hair.

KFC restaurant.description: Interior scene showing a red tray with a KFC meal including fries, a burger, and a drink, followed by customers eating at a table with orange juice, one in a green shirt and white jacket, the other in a pink scarf. The exterior features a large window advertisement for 'THE PLUS CHICKEN BURGER OF KFC'. A male voice with an accent is heard speaking about KFC having a 'special touch' that 'changes everything' compared to other restaurants, providing an audio context to the visual setting.

Taco Bell.description: Exterior with purple and white horizontal slats and a bell-shaped logo, which a female narrator states 'has not entered at all' in France.

Carl’s Jr..description: Exterior with a yellow star logo and an 'OUVERTURE LE 18 JANVIER' sign, with a female narrator explaining it 'is playing catch-up after breaking ground on its first French location in 2023'.

Wendy’s.description: Exterior sign with its red-haired girl logo, which a female narrator states 'is supposedly planning a move too' in France.

Gourmet dish.description: A metal dome being lifted to reveal a pile of seasoned meat on a tortilla, presented on a black slate, while a male voice with a French accent mentions potential menu items like 'a burger, a beef, a chicken, and maybe the tacos in the future'.

Chipotle Mexican Grill.description: Exterior storefront with beige walls, large windows, and a red and black circular logo, noted by a female narrator as having 'only six locations so far' in France.

Steak 'n Shake.description: Exterior with a large 'STEAK 'N SHAKE' sign and outdoor diners, which a female narrator states 'now boasts 37 locations' in France.

CNBC.description: Logo displayed prominently during the end credits and final screen, with a colorful peacock design and text 'CNBC' in blue, followed by production credits and a call to action.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a woman wearing a green and white patterned headscarf and a green face mask, standing in a protest crowd with a Palestinian flag visible, as a narrator discusses the "high Muslim population." The scene transitions to a wider shot of a Parisian street, showing a protester waving a Palestinian flag at a traffic light amidst other demonstrators holding signs like 'URGENCE PALESTINE', while the narration continues, highlighting France's large Muslim population. The camera then cuts to a McDonald’s storefront, defaced with graffiti and protest signs like 'BOYCOTT ISRAEL', as a graphic overlay confirms "7% France’s share of McDonald’s total revenue," aligning with the narrator's statement about France's contribution to the fast-food chain's revenue. Inside the McDonald’s, a customer interacts with a self-service kiosk displaying 'TOUCHEZ POUR COMMANDER', while the narrator explains McDonald's blamed weaker performance on "pricing backlash," acknowledging a sales drop in the US. The scene shifts to a Subway restaurant exterior with its iconic green and yellow sign, as a male voice with a French accent comments on "stringflation," noting how "they reduce the size of the burger and they raise the price." The narrator then reveals that "Subway closed more than 100 of its French storefronts since 2015," while the Subway exterior remains visible. Andrew Charles, Managing Director at TD Cowen, is then seen speaking in a professional setting, explaining that "the more near-term risk is going to be more macro-oriented that France seems to be the first European market that's really succumbing to restaurant industry traffic and restaurant industry traffic softness." The video returns to the McDonald’s exterior, showing pedestrians and then inside where customers use self-service kiosks displaying 'VOS BURGERS PRÉFÉRÉS, ÉGALEMENT DISPONIBLES AVEC DU BACON', as the narrator comments on the trickiness of "staying competitive in the new digital age." Joel Tissier, CEO of Domino’s Pizza France, is then shown speaking indoors, stating, "We come from a world where before 2016, the only people that were delivering were pizzas and sushis. I compete with pizzas on one hand, so I need to be..." This is followed by a shot of stacked Domino’s pizza boxes with the slogan 'TAKE A FRESH LOOK'.

The video opens with a close-up of a pizza topped with pepperoni, cheese, and green peppers, followed by a Domino’s Pizza box being opened, revealing the brand’s logo. Joel Tissier, CEO of Domino’s Pizza France, is then seen speaking indoors, his professional male voice explaining Domino's strategy to be "best in class" in the delivery business, acknowledging competitors like McDonald's and BK are "seven and twelve times bigger" than them. The scene shifts to an exterior shot of a Burger King restaurant in Paris, with pedestrians and a Théâtre du Caveau de la République sign. A female narrator's voice details how not everyone welcomes these expansions, with a protest sign against McDonald’s in Montorgueil ('Non au fast food géant dans la rue Montorgueil!') and a Change.org petition against a Burger King in Place Martin Nadaud, Paris 20, displayed, showing 24,932 signatures and a quote from Collectif Place Martin Nadaud: 'No to the promotion of outdated environmental practices'. The narrator continues, explaining that France implemented new laws in the same year, targeting fast-food companies over waste, requiring QSRs to offer reusable cutlery and cut down on single-use packaging. A Guardian article headline 'Ban on single-use restaurant tableware hailed as fast-food ‘revolution’ in France' appears, followed by scenes of people eating fast food at outdoor tables, including a woman with curly hair and glasses at a Burger King, and another person at a Five Guys restaurant. A different male voice, likely an industry expert, discusses creating "dishes especially for the QSR brown restaurants" and how "sustainability seems better than the others." Upbeat, light background music begins as the video ends with a shot of a Five Guys restaurant interior, where customers are ordering milkshakes and employees in red shirts are working behind the counter, while the female narrator concludes that "The QSR market in France has a lot more room to grow still," mentioning "Chipotle, for example."

The video opens with exterior shots of American fast-food chains in France, beginning with a Chipotle Mexican Grill storefront, which a female narrator notes "has only six locations so far." This transitions to a Taco Bell, which the narrator states "has not entered at all." A close-up then shows a gourmet dish being prepared, with a metal dome lifted to reveal seasoned meat on a tortilla, as a male voice with a French accent mentions potential menu items like "a burger, a beef, a chicken, and maybe the tacos in the future." Next, a Steak 'n Shake restaurant is shown with patrons dining outdoors, with the narrator adding it "now boasts 37 locations." A Carl’s Jr. location, marked 'OUVERTURE LE 18 JANVIER' (Opening January 18), appears, with the narrator explaining it "is playing catch-up after breaking ground on its first French location in 2023." A Wendy’s sign is briefly seen, with the narrator stating it "is supposedly planning a move too." The scene cuts to Andrew Charles, Managing Director at TD Cowen, in an interview, discussing how "the next push is going to be more of a focus on chicken," aiming for "higher margin" and a "younger consumer focused" strategy. This is followed by a Domino’s Pizza storefront and an employee placing a pizza box, while a male voice with a French accent discusses making their product "broadly available," having "close to 500 stores," and mentioning "the pizza distributor." The narrator then projects the industry to be worth "about 21.5 billion euros by 2028," concluding that "the French palate seems ready for it." The video ends with a KFC meal being carried and customers eating, including a woman in a green shirt and another in a pink scarf, as a male voice with a French accent remarks, "The taste is really different."

The video opens inside a KFC restaurant with a close-up of two individuals enjoying a meal, accompanied by a light, upbeat background music. One person, wearing a green shirt with 'MNMM SINCE 2010' and a white jacket, eats fried chicken, while the other, in a dark jacket and pink scarf, sips orange juice. A male voice with an accent comments, "in front of uh the other restaurants. I think uh they have a special touch and that changes everything," as the red tray between them displays KFC-branded packaging, fries, and a burger. The scene then cuts to the person in the green shirt and white jacket, now sitting alone at an outdoor table in front of the KFC storefront, which advertises 'THE PLUS CHICKEN BURGER OF KFC'. The camera focuses on a red tray with a KFC meal as hands reach in to eat, with the background music continuing. The video transitions to the CNBC logo, followed by end credits on a white background listing production details including 'Produced and Edited by Natalie Rice', 'Camera by Federico Campanale', and 'Animation by Christina Locopo'. The final frames display the CNBC logo again with a call to action: 'Let us know what you think in the comments below', alongside 'Subscribe' and 'FIRST IN BUSINESS WORLDWIDE,' against a dark blue cityscape backdrop. The segment, published in March 2024, includes additional footage from various sources like Getty, AP Photos, Reuters, Domino’s Pizza, Chipotle, TikTok, IBISWorld, and McDonald’s.

These visuals directly support Joel Tissier's discussion of the successful Raclette campaign.

This visual provides context for McDonald's long-standing presence in France, which is further discussed by the narrator and Andrew Charles.

A female narrator confirms Burger King 'boasts the second highest number of American QSR franchises in the country.'

global_entity_library.Subway restaurant: Exterior with green and yellow 'SUBWAY' sign, with people entering and exiting, seen as a male voice with a French accent comments on 'stringflation' and a narrator states Subway closed over 100 French storefronts since 2015.
global_entity_library.Protest signs on McDonald’s: Red and white signs with text 'BOYCOTT ISRAEL' and 'BOYCOTT CETTE ENSEIGNE' affixed to a McDonald’s storefront, some with red paint resembling blood, seen as a narrator discusses France's contribution to the fast-food chain's revenue and McDonald's blaming weaker performance on 'pricing backlash'.
global_entity_library.CNBC news headline overlay: White text on a dark background reading 'McDonald’s and Starbucks blame the Israel-Hamas war for slower sales — and the recovery might take a while', attributed to Amelia Lucas, February 5, 2024, appearing while a female voice discusses the restaurant industry's volatility and boycotts.
global_entity_library.Defaced McDonald’s sign: The McDonald’s logo with red handprints and paint splatters, appearing as if bloodied, with a darkened storefront in the background, shown as a female voice discusses the impact of boycotts on sales.
global_entity_library.Woman in green headscarf and mask: Wearing a green and white patterned headscarf and a green face mask, standing in a protest crowd with a Palestinian flag visible, seen as a narrator discusses France's 'high Muslim population'.
global_entity_library.Protest sign against McDonald’s: White banner with a red 'X' over the McDonald’s logo and French text 'Non au fast food géant dans la rue Montorgueil!', hanging from a green metal archway, reflecting public sentiment against fast food expansion.
global_entity_library.The Guardian article headline: Text overlay reading 'Ban on single-use restaurant tableware hailed as fast-food ‘revolution’ in France', attributed to Angelique Chrisafis, December 28, 2022, highlighting new regulations.
global_entity_library.Change.org petition: Online petition titled 'Un Burger King place Martin Nadaud, Paris 20' with 24,932 signatures, started by Collectif Place Martin Nadaud, displayed on a screen with a photo of the location, quoted by a female narrator as protesting 'outdated environmental practices'.
global_entity_library.Taco Bell: Exterior with purple and white horizontal slats and a bell-shaped logo, which a female narrator states 'has not entered at all' in France.
Exterior with a yellow star logo and an 'OUVERTURE LE 18 JANVIER' sign, with a female narrator explaining it 'is playing catch-up after breaking ground on its first French location in 2023'.
global_entity_library.Wendy’s: Exterior sign with its red-haired girl logo, which a female narrator states 'is supposedly planning a move too' in France.
global_entity_library.Chipotle Mexican Grill: Exterior storefront with beige walls, large windows, and a red and black circular logo, noted by a female narrator as having 'only six locations so far' in France.
global_entity_library.Steak 'n Shake: Exterior with a large 'STEAK 'N SHAKE' sign and outdoor diners, which a female narrator states 'now boasts 37 locations' in France.
global_entity_library.CNBC: Logo displayed prominently during the end credits and final screen, with a colorful peacock design and text 'CNBC' in blue, followed by production credits and a call to action.
